sourceMovie.title: Death Note
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Death Note (2006) stuck because it commits to its fantasy premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Shusuke Kaneko's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The fantasy and mystery blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Number 23
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Death Note clicked for you, The Number 23 (2007) is a strong next stop: both trade in mystery and thriller, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Death Note?
faq.answer: Try The Number 23, The Accountant², The Client—each shares DNA with Death Note while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Death Note?
faq.answer: Death Note (2006) is best described as Fantasy, Mystery, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Death Note part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Death Note Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
